True story, the very first, when I graduated from college, I went to work for my father. And the very first day, at the end of the day, he had a little office in the back of our menswear store. He called me into his office and he said to me, Bill, I just want you to know that, you know, I know it's your first day here and you're probably worried about it. I'm going to work you to death, but I just want to know, all I'm expecting is a half a day's workout at you. What you do the other 12 hours is up to you. And that's how for 20 some years, that was my repertoire. So anyway, so we will be ending at 9 o'clock tonight and then you have your own for this evening. Tomorrow morning, we will be back here at 8 o'clock. And at 8 o'clock, I've got a few announcements for you. Then bring your cell phones tomorrow morning because Spencer Brennan will be here from Boomtex and he'll be doing a special thing with your cell phones to get you a really, really, really cool gift that you already know what it is, but you don't know what it is. So you've heard about it and it's been promised to you, but you don't realize that. Actually, well, you might have, I don't know, but you're probably not going to be able to guess it. If you can guess it, you'll get the really cool gift. If you can't guess it, you'll still get the really cool gift. I'll run over everything for you. So if we could put the slides up on the side there. Managing the seminar game. This is part 2. So I'll explain that to you very much at the end. And this will be the only opportunity to get that. So you got to stay to the end. So how you like that, I'm sort of like punishing you if you don't stay to the end. So here, number one is the seven chief factors that affect seminar attendance. Here we go. Number one, actually, we'll do this as the sort of David Letterman format, we'll be counting backwards. Number seven is mindset of the list. So what is the mindset of the list? In the last couple of years, given the down economy, the truth of the matter is in many cases it's been harder to get people to come to seminars. Most of the people that I personally consult with and are running seminars, they're finding it more difficult to fill their seminars because the down economy is very much on people's minds. So they're saying, gee, maybe I shouldn't be spending the money to come to seminars. The truth of the matter is it's probably more important in the down economy to actually find ways to improve your business or also ways that you didn't even know about that you could be improving your business or finding the business within the business. But that doesn't change the fact that people do have a mindset of why they should not be coming to seminars from different times of the years. So one of the number seven factors, the mindset of the list of your prospects that you are actually communicating with. Number six is marketing funnel. Most people underestimate the difficulty of the job and their marketing funnel is way too small in order to get people to come to events at Glaser County. So just this event alone, over 90 different marketing touches. Many of them are online, they're Warners Costly, some of them are offline, some of the offline stuff isn't as costly, some of it is very, very costly, but over 90 touches. So most people don't begin to do enough marketing to get people to come to those events. And frankly, we do that battle every day with our independent business advisors. I know that in the room there's many, many great independent business advisors, but we do that battle every day with our independent business advisors to get people to come to their local chapters. They don't begin to do enough marketing. There's not enough of a marketing funnel. And with that, there's two things that affect that dramatically, is diversity of the media. So not just using one media. If you're just using online, you're crazy. If you're just using email marketing online, you're crazy. So it's the diversity of the media. And the second thing is lead time. Is are you giving yourself sufficient amount of time to do the marketing? We typically at Glaser County, when we go into full force in marketing event, we give ourselves six months lead time in order to do the job the right way. Six months, we think, in terms of. And those of you that are looking around the room that I have done some consulting with, you've heard me talk about this over and over again, is not doing enough of it. Also looking around the room, I don't want to point anybody out, so I'm trying not to make eye contact. Those of you in the room, I always look around, who give them, do enough diversity, do enough, because I know them, do enough diversity, give themselves enough lead time, and do enough marketing, they have very successful events, and they've learned that. So there's that point of fact, the marketing funnel itself, and the two key factors are diversity, not putting all your eggs in one basket. And number two is your lead time to fill it. Number five is size of the list. Obviously, the more people that you got, that you can invite, the better chance you got. It's a numbers game. So the size of the list. So everything sort of always moves back down to at the end of the day to build the list. We're all in the list building business. We should all be in the list building, but it's for many reasons that we should always be building our list constantly. And including the fact that you always will lose prospects and customers, so you have to constantly replace them as well. So there's list building, which is the reason why tomorrow we are doing the herd building day to teach you ways to build a list, and also to care for the list. Number four is knowledge about the list. And this is really important because this allows you to do segmentation. The best result that you can get in any marketing that you do is the result that the person who's receiving your message says to themselves, hey, this person is speaking to me. This person is speaking directly to me. So there is seminar attendees versus nine attendees. That's one way to think about segment your list. Number two is customers versus prospects, whether they've bought from you before or never bought from you before. Location of people on the list. So are they close to where the event is or are they coming from far distances? Contact information that we have about them. Do we have their email address, their email address? Do we have, can we text them? Can we fax them? Can we call them by phone? Pick up the phone and call them. The more of that we have, the more media that we can actually use. Previous purchase history. So what do we know about them? Have they bought a product in the past? So like for example, if somebody were to go to our resource center and buy the A to Z Blueprint seminar, they are raising their hand and they are saying the fact that I'm interested in information marketing. So those people should be getting a separate message, which is in our case they do, a separate message telling them that, hey, we notice that you invested this. Obviously you're interested in information marketing. If you want to be at the premier event in the country where the best information marketers come to once a year, then this is for you. So knowing about their purchase history makes it infinitely easier to get people to say, gee, this person is speaking to me. Whether they are a previous seminar attendees, and that is different than the first bullet. So first bullet is whether they go to any seminars or not. And that next to last bullet on here is about whether they are a previous seminar attendee at this event. So for example, those of you in the room who have been to an info summit in the past, if you didn't yet register for this info summit but you're in the room, you probably got somewhere late in the funnel a request because you're an alumni of the info summit, you got a special alumni mailing. The next one is whatever you know about them, whatever you know about them. I will tell you that what happens to me a lot is one of my info mastermind members calls me up on the phone in trauma. I mean, this happens to me a lot. Bill, I'm doing this event. It's five weeks from today. Okay? It's five weeks from today. I've got a room that will hold 100 people and I got three registered. Okay? That's what I hear. I hear it a lot. Okay? But how do I solve this problem? I always go right to this number four. I said, well, what do we know about your list? And then we create, we craft different offers for different people on the list. This is how I win that battle. So the knowledge you know about the list is major important for you. And it's the same way in all your marketing, whether it's for seminars or not, is what you know about the list. When I was in the menswear business, that's how I won the battle there was segmenting my list. Number three is relevant topic or theme, relevant topic or theme. Really, really important there, which is why it's number three. Because again, I'm moving that bump in order of importance. So coming with something that is relevant or a theme that is really relevant. You have to and Dan said it last night and I would sort of really echo it. You can't keep doing the same thing over and over again and kept expecting people to keep coming back. We do as Dan pointed out last night, we do two national events a year. One is our super conference and the other is our info summit. But if we made them the same every time, if we made them the same every time, we would not get rooms filled with people coming. We're constantly coming up with new, new, fresh ideas. We're going to have how we're actually making them new and fresh. As a matter of fact, our super conference, we're just about putting the polishing touches on it, which all of you will hear about in the upcoming days or weeks or whenever when we get everything out to you. And you'll see it has a new whole coat of paint on it versus anything that we've ever done before. And so therefore, once again, is making a relevant topic or theme that is different than before, giving it a reason for existence is so important. Number two is being influential. That's number two is being influential. If those of you who do not have Dan's influential writing workshop, you are just short changing yourselves. It's actually the influential writing and communication workshop teaching you how to be a person of influence. The job of the sale and any sale that you have becomes infinitely easier if you are someone who is influential, where somebody wants to be with you because of the fact that you are someone who is influential. So being influential is important. And everything that we do at Glazer Kennedy is all built on that platform of being people of influence. Number one is relationship with a list. Does a wooden list know you? Do they trust you? If they know and trust you, that is the number one thing you have to have. And the reason why I say that is because I'm always seeing how people try to go into the seminar business without doing all this stuff. What they do is they figure this is, you know, they come into a room like this here and they see 800 people here. And if they do the math, so somebody average paid $2,000 to be here, so 2,000 times $800, and you say, gee, this is an easy business. I'm going to just do this business, okay? They don't realize that what it takes to do that. So therefore you have to really earn the right, and I've written articles about it, to earn the right for being able to sell things to people. And that's really what this is all about, is earning the right and having the relationship with the list and being influential are the two key factors that gives you earning the right in order to ask people to do business with you. So those are the seven key factors that affect seminar attendance. Okay, next thing is business booster, seminar business boosters. And with this is where you're going to see, I forgot what I said, but I think it was 39 different examples. And so I'm going to show you examples of each of these. Number one is repurpose with power, repurposing content. I'll explain to you what I do with that each year. Celebrity is a big booster. Dan talked about that last night. And again, it's a big booster. List segmentation, I talked about that already. I'll show you some examples of that. Diverse media, I talked about diversity. I'm going to show you examples of diversity in media. Demonstration is something that nobody hardly ever talks about. Something for the first time I'm talking about to you all is demonstrating things by getting people to register. P and C is personality and clever. So using personality and clever stuff. So examples for you to S and D. And the next one is cool, a.k.a. stuff. So examples to S and D and swipe and flout. So the last little category is cool stuff. So with that said, let's get started to repurpose with power. What do I mean by repurpose with power? What do we do at Glaser Kennedy and what do I suggest that you do? What we do at Glaser Kennedy is we start with our main piece of marketing. We start with our main piece of marketing and we always build that. Quite frankly, that is the biggest job that we do in our marketing is we first start with our main piece of marketing. How do we do that? We build a multi-page sales letter that we put everything in there. Everything in this multi-page sales letter. And from that multi-page sales letter, we repurpose everything out of that. So we create this multi-page sales letter and we repurpose everything out of that. So for example, here is the front cover of a sales letter that I think was for the two years ago in the Post Summit. The very front cover, you got this. Somebody's, this was personalized with the person's name, open immediately to find how you can attend a $5,000 copyrighted boot camp with Dan Kennedy, Bill Glaser for free. And there was a whole front of the cover of the newsletter with Suzanne Summer, who was at that time our celebrity entrepreneur that we had invited to speak at that event. And so that's page number one. Page number two moves into this, we're rolling out the red carpet and inviting you. Now it's important you pay attention to that because you're going to see that again soon. That whole page right there, we're rolling out the red carpet and we're inviting you. I'm going to show you how we repurpose that. Then there's page three where we start talking about the speakers. Page four, we start talking more about the Info Summit. Page five keeps more about the Info Summit, show a speaker in this particular case, it was Karen Schaefer. Then we had Timothy Swords speaks. We talked about him and then we had a Mr. X and then we had Ryan Dice speak and then we had Matt Basak speak and then we had Clayt Mass speak and then we had Ari Galper. And then we went on and on and what else do we got here? We talked about the details and I talked about the A to Z Blueprint seminar and the 17 blueprints and it goes on and on. And then we had another insert inside with a free gift which was the copywriting seminar that we did at the same seminar. So there was a major sales that I'm going through real fast. You all will get an opportunity to get a complete copy of everything that I show you here at the end. So you're not going to have time, I don't have time to read it all because I want to get as much content in these 90 minutes as I can. But it's a full sales letter. So then how do we repurpose that? Then after that, success stories, there. Now this is the website repurposed. So this was the page you came to at the website and you just sort of identified yourself by your level of membership. So you clicked on the ticket that identified you, the level of membership. And then it went right to the same sales letter that I just showed you that was offline. So we're rolling out the red carpet and this went online. It scrolled on down all the way to the end of the sales letter that I just showed you offline. We repurposed it for our online sales letter. Today we do a lot of video sales letters as all of you probably have seen. Our video sales letters are repurposed. I take the same content that I do in my big main sales letter. A big main sales letter. And I actually turned that into a video sales letter where the text comes up on screen as somebody is actually reading it. So it's the exact same thing. How do I do that? What I do is I take my main sales letter, the main print of the sales letter, and I get out two really valuable instruments that are hard to find, but you can find if you search enough for them, it's a ruler and a pen. Those two things. And I put lines under each section that I want to cut out to make a message that I use. So like for example, that is my online sales letter. Then I start moving to emails. And I'll take the sales letter and I'll chop it into, in this particular case, 18 emails. I took my main sales letter and I chopped it into 18 emails that I distribute over usually six weeks. Over six weeks we send out 18 emails, each one with a piece that came right out of the main sales letter. So that's how I repurpose it. What's important about that, why I tell you that, is because everybody thinks the job of writing the copy, they make it harder than it needs to be. The job of writing the copy is to create your main big sales letter. Main, main big sales letter. Then after you do that, then you repurpose that and use it as many ways as you possibly can. Everybody get that? Got it? Yes? Right. It really isn't as difficult as you think it is. It is difficult to write that main sales letter. But once you got that, the rest of it is now just repurposing it and moving forward with it. So there's a couple of emails that are chopped out of there. Okay, the second thing is celebrity. Now Dan talked about it last night and I just can't echo it enough. Most people are really cheap about this and that's really poor thinking. Dan called it, what did he call it last night? Shamu the whale, right? Shamu the whale. I couldn't wait to call Cal and tell him he was Shamu the whale. Ivanka Trump loved being known as Shamu the whale. But that's really what this really is all about, is you want to get somebody who, that person within themselves, although they're expensive, Cal did not come here for free as much as I'd like him to have. George Foreman never came for free, Ivanka didn't come for free. Gene Simmons was the most expensive person I have ever gotten to come to Glaser Kenny stage. He was major expensive. None of these people came for free, but it didn't matter to me. Because it all comes down to is by them being there, how many more seats do I have to sell to justify them being there? And in all cases, they will justify that expense. Gene Simmons without a doubt put 200 people into the Glaser Kenny seminar the year he spoke by him being there because people came that would never normally have come to a Glaser Kenny seminar. We know that for a fact and then many of them once they experience Glaser Kenny seminars, they'll keep coming back over and over and over again. So the celebrity is a key thing and you really want to be thinking in terms of finding the right celebrities, but also at the same time it's relevant celebrities. So as Dan Kennedy said last night, is we don't just get a celebrity, we also bring one that has a relevancy. So always looking for somebody who's like a entrepreneur celebrity as well. So like for example, the year that we brought... Well, you know, Street's company, you know, so we just Photoshopped, thank God for Photoshopped, you know, Suzanne Summers was coming, so we Photoshopped Dan and Bill. By the way, please notice Dan's right arm and the placement of it, but it was how we used it. Now that is a... that does the same thing that the celebrity on the infomercial at 2 o'clock in the morning when you are turning channels and you see a celebrity makes you stop and say, what is whoever... whoever celebrity is on at that time, what is this person doing on the air at 2 o'clock in the morning? And what this person does, the male or female celebrity, is it makes you stop changing the channels and stop and look at it. That's the same thing the celebrity does, is what is Suzanne Summers doing with Dan Kennedy and Bill Glazer? What is Suzanne Summers doing at a Glazer Kennedy event? It is something that makes you stop and look at the marketing. So that's the one that we use with Suzanne Summers. This is one that Rory Fatt used famous Amos, the chocolate chip cookie guy. It's a perfect fit for him. He's in a restaurant business, so it's all about food. Celebrity makes you stop and look. Here's one, the year that we used Ivanka Trump. We actually... I spread it out for you, but that's actually a holiday greeting card that we created with putting Ivanka Trump in Times Square up on this big, whatever, billboard in Times Square. So it's happy holidays from Times Square. We photoshopped in Ivanka Trump on that billboard. That really is Times Square. You open up inside and you see Wishing You a Peace and Prosperity, the holiday season. It's from Dan, Bill and Ivanka. And then on the left-hand side, it was a promotion for our super... I think that was our super conference. Now, it's disreputable to be using holidays for doing this kind of thing, and that's why we're so successful because of our disreputableness. But we use that whenever we can. We're entering the conversation that's on their head around holiday times and sending out a holiday card, which is one of the things that we've often done. So again, Ivanka Trump makes you stop and look at what is Ivanka Trump doing on the card that I send out? The next category is list segmentation. Segmenting the list. So how are all the different ways that I can segment my list? I showed you a bunch of stuff before to think about. Let me just show you a couple of examples now. So like, for example, this is a cover letter that went out that we used... We put it on top of the sales letter that we sent out for Dan Kennedy's seven-figure seminar. I think we held that last year, seven-figure seminar. How to go from six to seven figures in income. What this is, if you actually read this, and I'll just sort of give you the message, these are people who attended another Dan Kennedy high-end seminars. And what we did is we created this cover letter that went out to them, telling them, because you went to, I think in this particular case, I might be wrong, but I think it was the influential writing workshop, the live one. Because you attended that, I think you're also going to be interested in this. This is an example of going back at their purchase history, based on what we know about them, to invite them to come to an event that it's a clearly targeted message to them. So this screw surviving cover letter, we had about eight different versions of this, depending on what your previous buying history was. Everybody got the letter, but it had different copying depending on what you did in the past. This one, the Zoom Zoom one, Dan showed you a version of this last night, but this is the original Zoom Zoom one. This is actually, usually inside, there's a little toy car inside, and we mail this out to people that are within driving distance of the event. And we say copy in there along the lines of, because of the fact that people are coming from far places, such as Japan and Australia and New Zealand and the United Kingdom, and they're spending thousands of dollars to fly here, just imagine the fact that you can just get into your car and drive on over to the seminar. So we have a special opportunity for you because you live within driving distance. That is list segmentation, which by again, by the way, I'm not going through everything with you, as far as these events, these pieces, but again, on the CD that you can get at the end, everything is on there for you to actually swipe and deploy for your businesses. If I went through each one of you with you, we would be here for like three days, so I'm just giving you the highlights of it. This is another one, these are for people that live outside the United States. This was the free airfare and clothes piece with the international boarding pass that's printed on the outside of it. This is a special message that goes to people who live outside the United States. This is list segmentation, and it makes a big, big difference in how people respond. This one is an interesting one in the list segmentation department, which is actually an endorsed mailing. So like for example, this is a mailing that Infusion mailed out to all their customers saying to them, because you are a customer of Infusion, I'd like to invite you to come to the seminar that I'm speaking at, which in this particular case, Clay Mass was speaking at, and also at that seminar, we did a special training for Infusion users. That's a specialized, segmented mailing that we mailed out to other folks. So again, the question that you need to be thinking to yourself is, what information do I know about my list? How can I slice and dice this information in order to make it relevant to the thing that I am actually offering folks? Again, I just want to tell you something. If I was up here right now not talking about information marketing, not talking about seminar marketing, and if I was talking to a group of restaurant owners, if I was talking to a group of mortgage brokers, if I was talking to a group of dentists, God forbid I was talking to a group of lawyers, all those things together, I would be teaching them the same thing. The way you win the battle is by matching your message to your specific market, so that people say to themselves, this person is speaking to me. Okay, next is diverse media. So, and again, I have a lot of, in my mastermind groups, I have a lot of these guys that are pure internet marketers. Thank God not all of them, but a lot of them. They do outnumber the real estate investing guys, especially in this economy. But I have a lot of them in there, and I have this battle with them all the time. They want to completely win the battle of filling their seminars, but just sending out emails. And I'm telling you that the ones that listen to me, they get this, that they don't just send out emails. I mean, so like, I'm looking over there, Lloyd Irving is sitting over there, okay? So Lloyd doesn't have this problem. Lloyd is very well recognized for lots of things, but one of the things he's well recognized for is the fact that he's a very, very bright internet marketer. But when you listen to everything that he does, to make, he just did a seminar for people in the martial arts business. He used all kinds of media, not just online stuff. See, he doesn't fall prey to that. The ones that use diversity in their media, they always will win this battle over the people that just use one kind of media. And I'm picking on email marketing, it could be direct mail. You don't want to just use one thing. So let me show you a couple examples. So first of all, here's newsletter's inserts. By the way, this should be familiar to you. This is a repurposed product. We took the main sales letter and pulled out of their segments that we put as newsletter inserts in our NoBS marketing letter and also some of our other newsletters each month. It's a repurposed product, but we put inserts in our newsletters. That's diversity. Here's another one, our online TV show, our online media. We promote our events on our online TV show. Here's another one, which is our NoBS Not-So-Daily Easeem. It's another place that we promote our events. Here's another one, this is faxing. So this is actually a fax that we send, the two-page fax that we send out. And we use fax very sparingly because it is the one media that more people get upset about than any other media that we do. So we don't overuse it. We just use it sparingly, but when we use it, it's still very effective. By the way, there's a lesson here about the two-page fax that I want to just stop and tell you about, which is the fact that page number one is the message and page number two is the order form. And it's really important whenever you do faxing is you always do a second page, which includes the order form. And the reason why is because when you do this, they don't have the order form in front of them if you don't fax them the order form. So they have to now go and find the order form and or they have to go online to register. You're making it difficult for them. They are at the fax machine, fax them two pages, fax them in order form. So while they're at that fax machine, they can fill it out and fax it back. Let them use the media that brought them to the party to actually respond to it. So that's two-page fax machine fax. Whenever you're doing fax, always use two pages and always send out the order form with it. Here's a voice broadcast. Now, this is the text, but this is a voice broadcast. So we use voice broadcasts, another type of media. We use it more often than we use faxing, but we don't overuse it. What I like using in voice broadcasts, we broadcast is I like using celebrity impersonators. I'm not sure which one this is. Oh, this is Superman. So we actually did a whole script on the whole Superman thing, look up in the sky, it's a bird, it's a plane, that kind of thing. So we like to do things that are kind of cute with it because people enjoy getting on their voice, their answer machines. They enjoy getting more personality and so they don't get upset about it. They sort of say they laugh about it. So again, use outrageous advertising, which I teach in all your voice broadcasts. Newspaper advertising. So this is one that actually, I don't know if Dave, Dave are you in the room? There he is. This is one that Dave is in my Info Mastermind group. This is one that he actually uses for Canada and which he puts a post-it note on the outside of the newspaper that he does all the marketing for this event. And so he does a post-it note in the outside of the newspaper talking about the event and then actually inside he also has an ad in there that's also talking about the event. So what he usually does, doesn't have to, they both stand on their own, but he usually has a little post-it note driving them to the big ad inside the paper. Very, very effective for local events is to use newspaper advertising still. And the good news is that it's become much, much less expensive to use it because those guys are traumatized right now by being a difficult media. So you can negotiate real hard with those folks. Postcards. I like using postcards. This is kind of a cool postcard. This is one, I think this is a Roy Fat postcard and on the other side of it he actually included the enrollment form on the other side of the postcard. So this is one side where he had his message and on the other side it's the actual form that you fill out and just send it right back. So it's right on the postcard. So postcards are a good type of media. This one is a Magalaw. This is actually a Magalaw. We usually do one of these every year. This is DVDs, another type of media to send out. So this is a DVD mailing that we would send out. So again, another type of media. Some people will go online, some people will read things, other people want to watch things. So this is a mailing where we sent out which had a DVD inside. So inside that is a DVD. CD mailing. This is the best example of S&D you will have ever seen. I created the CD mailing for my BGS marketing system and Rory Fatt knocked it off exactly to the T. Every word that I wrote he rewrote for his own business. But this is sending out a little booklet with a sales letter inside and a CD in there because some people will listen. I personally, if I had to pick one or the other, as far as which one I would use, I would use CDs over DVDs if I had to. But that's only if I had to. What I prefer to do is use them both. I want to use every type of media I can. Why do I pick CDs over DVDs? Because it's easier for people to consume CDs than to watch DVDs. It's because you can put them in your car as you're driving around. In my car I've always got a policy that's in there so I'm using it sort of like it's a traveling university. They always have me learn more. Demonstration. This is the one I talked about that very seldom anybody ever talks about. This is a biggie if you want like a real, real big takeaway. If you're looking for something that you want to do that very few other people are doing. Demonstration is really, really important. Actually Doug, where's Doug? Our cameraman. Are you in the room? Demonstrating the fact this is the right thing to do. Infomercials, when you watch infomercials, they are a total demonstration. They're demonstrating the products that they are selling. That's a demonstration. And anytime they do demonstration of the use of the product, the efficiency of the product, they always get better sales. And that same thing happens in seminar stuff. So like for example, here's a piece. Somebody actually talked about the fact they ripped me off on this. But this is a, I think this might be this year, I'm not sure. So this is a seminar survey to find out what they want letter. This is a real shortcut for a lot of folks. Is if you want to create a seminar, and I teach this a lot of different places, this is basically my three steps system that I use and I teach to get whatever you want sold, sold infinitely easier. Step number one is to survey your list. Step number two is to report back results to them of the survey. And step number three is to give them what they want, which in this particular case, the results in this case is these are the things that I wanted people to speak at at the seminar. So these are the topics that you chose that you wanted. So now we've built this seminar for you. Now this works in any business. This doesn't have to be in the seminar business. We use the three step survey, which is about any business that I now do consulting with, but it works really, really well in seminars. So you survey the list, you report back results. Usually when you report back results, you also give them a gift for filling it out. In this particular case, a year we did it, we gave them a t-shirt. And then we also then did our whole sales letter built on the premise that this is what you asked for, so we're giving you now what you asked for. It's a pretty advanced strategy and loads of you that are looking for advanced stuff. This is a very smart strategy to use for your seminar marketing. This is the personalized weight with, personalized with don't jump facts. So this is their first name. It's on there with a great cartoon is weight don't jump. I've never seen anything outperform this in a fax. This is the keep them engaged mailing. This is kind of an interesting thing. So all of you got over the course of the last couple months from us, the CDs of the set of how successful entrepreneurs think to grow rich, right? You all got it, right? So it was a six CD set, and every month for three months you got two CDs, right? Right? And then on the fourth month, month, you also got a CD of our vendors, right? So why do we do that? So we do it really, I told you I was going to reveal stuff that I shouldn't reveal, how I'm manipulating you guys. We do it because we keep you engaged. A lot of people register for our events far in advance, and we want to keep them excited about the event. So over the course of time, usually about four months out from the event, I start sending out every month a mailing to everybody who's coming with information on there about the event. So here's how you register for the hotel, here's about other, if you want to add a guest, here's how you add a guest, on and on and on and on and on. Plus, here's a free gift for you. So again, advanced strategy for all of you, which is going to make it really tough for me to keep coming up with new stuff, but if I keep showing you everything, I'm going to do it for you. I think this is the last one I got showed you. I told you at 39 exhibits. This is the Homer letter. This is what happens is that the best time if you're doing an event and repeating it every year, the best time to sell that event. Is at that event for next year. That is the best time to sell it. When we left our super conference in 2010 this year for next year super conference, we left with 647 people registered for next year. That takes a true. Thank you. That takes that takes a tremendous amount of pressure off of the promoter to now have to win next year. I mean next year we, you know, I, 2011 super conference, I'm pretty certain we'll have 1200 people there again. But you know what? It's a lot easier to get now start with 600 and whatever 47 people than it is to start from zero. So we, so we, the best time to sell it if you're doing it again next year or whatever is to sell it at the actual event that you are at. So however, not everybody registers. Not everybody registered. So this is the home. This is the letter that I've sent out for everybody who didn't register. It goes out right away for everybody who didn't register when they left the event. And I've sent it out from various people. I've sent that once from Karen. It came from Karen, a neglected wife. Once it came from sharing my assistant, a beaten assistant. Once it came from, I think John Gibbs also, and this one came from my dog, Homer. So this was a letter that I sent out from my dog telling everybody that, that bills away right after the event. He's relaxing. He's resting, which is total nothing. I'm back at work Monday morning, but he's away. And before he gets back, if you still want to register for the event, and I can only think of three reasons why you didn't register. One reason is that you got so caught up in the event that you forgot to turn in the form. The second reason is, where are they now? The second reason is because of the fact that you're afraid of coming back next year because you won't implement everything that you set out to do. And what better way to hold yourself accountable than now registering for next year's event so you know that you've got to get it all done. And the third reason why you don't come back is something that I absolutely positively cannot remember right now, but when you get everything there, he will tell you the third reason. I can't remember what it is right now. So you have to have a reason why copy somebody did not register. Okay. Oh, the third reason why is because you left early. You had to leave early for some unknown reason. You had to get home. And so you weren't there when people actually offered you the opportunity to come back again the following year. So there was just three reasons why. So you have reason why copy. So you send that out and we always get people who did not register, who should have registered to fax in their registration. So this is a sort of insider trick for you all, which is going to make it infinitely easier, harder for me to keep doing this stuff on you guys in the future. Now all you have to do in order to just make my job easier, just register while you're here and then, you know, leave me alone. Okay. Okay. Several of our logistics. This is the painful but necessary stuff. The stuff that Dan Kennedy asked me personally to talk about, which I hate talking about it because it is painful, but let's talk about it. One, site selection. Where you're doing it at? What are the big things to think about? Number one thing is location. Dan is 100% right. It's best to keep people isolated. The fact that we are in downtown Baltimore, this beautiful hotel surrounded by these great restaurants and a beautiful waterfront is not the ideal location to do it at all. The fact that I can get in my car and drive 20 minutes to be here was a big help for that. But as most people know at Grazer Kenne events, we usually have met airport facilities where you're kind of isolated. So location, flight access, how easy it is to get a flight in. My favorite place to have an event that not because it's my favorite city, although it is one of my favorite cities, but my favorite place to have a seminar at is actually Chicago because it's about a two and a half hour flight from anywhere in the United States. It's easy to get to. There's two big airports there. Always lots of flights coming in and coming out. So it's my favorite place to have it, but you can't have it there every time. So we, you know, we move them around. But so the flight access, the best time of year. When is a good time of year to have it? Chicago was not particularly a good time to have an event in February or, you know, when, yeah. So like our event that we haven't the event that I'm doing with Matt Bay Sack, the JV affiliate event in January, we're having in Orlando. Good weather for that time of year. Site tour is really important. It is we have a crew led by Sherry. And when we, before we sign it on the dotted line that we're going to go to a hotel, we send out usually two of our people sharing somebody else to go visit the hotel to see the hotel, make sure everything is in good order at the hotel. So we do a site tour. Usually if you have to fly somewhere and your events large enough, they will pay for you to come out and do the site tour. It doesn't cost you any money other than your people's time. And the type of suites available for VIPs. So we have VIPs that come in all our big speakers and all that stuff. So we tried to get good suites for them. So they available. So those are site selection things to consider. Next is important terms of the clauses. They will not tell you this unless you ask for it. They hope you don't know anything. They really hope that you don't know. They are the enemy. I can tell you that right now. So internet rate clause, cancellation clause if you have to cancel, noise clause. I don't know if any of you have ever been in an event. But once I think Ron you were telling me this story. Once you were speaking somewhere and the alarm system went off or something like that. You probably, everything has happened somewhere where you've spoken at. So noise clause. Hotel concessions. What kind of concessions the hotels will give you. These are important terms of your contracts that you got to really, really negotiate hard. Next one is hidden fees. Hidden fees they don't have there. Shipping and receiving. You have no idea how much they can charge you for shipping and receiving. By the way, does anybody know how much a gallon of coffee is when we serve it here? What do you, $60 gallon of coffee in this crappy place here. What? Huh? That's right. That's right. I mean, so yeah, plus all the other extra stuff. So gallon of coffee. I mean, there's like no concess at all. AV requirements. Convention center fees if you're in a convention center. Types of rooms on the contract. So what are the types of rooms your guests get? In our particular case we always negotiate for our attendees to get the nicest rooms that they have. A ringing clause whether there's any ring costs because they will really rip you off unless you negotiate away from it. You know about the stuff and you actually negotiated out of your contract. Hotel bonuses to ask for. The meeting room rental fee. The meeting room rental fee that you're in right now is directly proportioned to how many rooms you fill in the hotel. So you can get these rooms oftentimes for free if you're filling up enough hotel rooms. 24 hour room rental. Whether or not you have access to the room for 24 hours or you have access for 12 hours, 16 hours. How long you have access to that room? Service fees waive. Whether you can get the service fees waive. Usually if you can't get them waived you can get a portion of them waived. Resort fees. I love that one. I can't tell you how many seminars I've spoken at as a speaker. And all of a sudden I go to check out and there's a resort fee on there for like 20 or 25 dollars. And all I did was went to the summer. I don't even know they had a resort at the place. Incentives to register for early room registration. This is kind of an interesting thing which we are now working on ourselves. Is whether if we can get our attendees to register before deadline where they get even a better rate. More and more hotels now are doing that for you. Vendor tips. Tips to deal with your vendors. Number one is local exhibitor decorators. You always will do better if you use the local exhibitor decorator than if you're using some big national firm. So you always want to try to find them. Usually the hotels are pretty good about giving you a list of those folks. The next thing is set up time, break down time during normal hours. When do we start moving in here at Glacier County? The event starts on Thursday. Now when we send our first crew out Monday. We start moving in on Monday. Every day we start adding more and more people that we're bringing here in order to get ready for this. So you have set up time and break down time. Enough time to actually get this event put together or any event that you do. Provide AV bids with agenda detail list of equipment. So they're AV folks. We love dealing with these guys back there. But we keep them honest. So before we come to any event we always have a group of folks that we actually give the bid out to. And show them a complete list of all the equipment that we need. So you want to do that as well because there's great diversity, great range that you can actually find. It would people will charge you for that. A discount from vendors for booking multiple events. So and the AV folks will usually give you discounts for multiple events. The hotels will give you discounts for multiple events. Marriott, Sheraton, Hyatt, Hilton's. If you say to them I'm doing three events in the next two years. Well they'll give you even better discounts for multiple events. So those are some vendor tips. Okay. So that was the dreaded stuff. I will tell you in terms of a hotel contract like this, going back and forth, Sherry going back and forth with them over and over, back and forth, back and forth. It probably takes us to negotiate the final lives of a contract usually around six weeks with all the back and forth. So it's not like you just get the hotel contract and you sign it. If you get the first hotel contract that they, if you sign the first hotel contract that they delivered to you and you sign it and send it back to them, what they do is they get their entire staff in a room and they all start laughing at you. Okay. So what you got to do is understand that they are testing you to see how much of this stuff you actually know. Okay. Seminar choreography to maximize profitability. So this is now choreographing the event. The event that you, similar to an event you're sitting in right now or any event you go to, you have to look at it as choreographing it. This is probably the single biggest thing that I do with people that I consult with in the seminar business where I help them to actually put on really very, very productive and profitable events. We always have a big session, never less than an hour to actually figure out how to choreograph the entire event. So here's the things we think about. Number one is making sure you deliver great value as soon as possible. Deliver great value as soon as possible. Who's the first speaker here yesterday? Dan Kennedy. Did he deliver great value? How many people, how many of you, well I want to ask, don't raise your hand. Many of you walked up to me after Sam Dan's presentation and said, if I just go home right now, I'm done. Most of you who didn't walk up to me were thinking that. Okay. Great value after that, Cal Ripken Jr., Mary Ellen Tribby. I mean great value early right away to deliver great value. So all of a sudden people said to themselves, I made the right decision to come here. So you got to deliver right up the bat great value as soon as possible. Number two, you got to make the list. You're number one, you're number two, number three, chief objectives of the seminar for the host. You got to make the list of what your three top objectives are. And you got to now choreograph the event to put number one first, number two second, number three third, as far as what you want to do. It is not a goal of a Glaser Kenny event, but a lot of people that I work with in niche markets, the biggest thing that they want to, their biggest objective is to sell coaching programs at their events. Obviously it's not a goal at Glaser Kenny events because we don't sell a lot of coaching programs. Even my Platinum program that I offered earlier today is only for some of you, it's not for all of you. It's not our chief goal. But a lot of events, the number one thing they want to sell is coaching at their events. That's their number one thing. So when we choreograph the whole agenda, it's all built around selling that thing. And then everything else takes a backseat to it, number two takes a backseat to it, second, number three, third. Diversity, non-duplication of topics. This thing is crazy. Okay. You wouldn't believe how many times speakers complain to me that they go to events and speak at it, and there's two other people on the agenda talking about the same thing they're talking about. It's crazy. So you can't have any duplication of topics. Number four is speaker introductions. Speaker introductions. How the speaker is introduced makes a gigantic difference in the choreography of the event. Most of us that are serious about putting on events, we always introduce the speakers ourselves. Chris, where's Chris? Chris Cardell. Chris Cardell is in Guinness Book of Records for being the best speaker, introducer of all times. He's got the, he's the book of record for that. He does the best introductions because Dan and I flew over to the UK. They never heard of Bill Glazer and Dan Cain in the UK enough. Okay. And by the time that he was done introducing us, we were kind of famous over there, you know? So speaker introductions are really, really important in how you, how you, how you introduce the speaker. And this also ties into being a person of influence and also being, having credibility and believability with your, your attendees. But the speaker and the introducer being the person of influence because he can transfer that influence over to now the speaker. The introducer, the host can do that. Speakers being respectful of other speakers. This is, those of you in the room, how many of you like to do a fair amount of speaking? A lot of speaking. Okay. So you like, no, you probably, you had an incidence on this where you've spoken on the stage and other speakers have tried to sabotage you. You know, we had one speaker years ago, some of you were probably there, was the most sabotaging speaker of all times I've ever seen. He has never been back on a Glacier Kennedy stage since that time. Really big sabotage. He'll, he'll say, he'll try to do anything at all to hurt the other speakers. So making sure speakers are being respectful of other speakers. Have you seen that at this event where you're at where all the speakers were respectful of everybody else? Everybody finished on time? Did you see all that? Nobody going over all this? Nobody talking badly about anybody? I won't even tolerate it, you know, at our stage. And it's important that that is thought about. If you don't know about that, you can get sabotaged by your speakers. If the speaker is offering a resource, God forbid. Okay. You have to think about who speaks before the speaker, who speaks after the speaker, this, when the, when the order forms are distributed, when the, how the order forms are collected, what the speaker's average closing percentage is elsewhere, and how speakers resources affect your number one, number two, and number three objective. So if you bring in a speaker, well that speaker's resource affects your main three objectives of what you're trying to accomplish at your events. So all that has to be thought out in the choreography of the events. Position of speakers to keep attendance throughout the seminar. So if you have, if you don't have speakers that are speaking throughout the seminar to keep attendance, so let me tell you a little bit of inside baseball. Do you know why Frank Kern is speaking tomorrow and not the first day? That is because he's positioned in a way, because everybody wants to hear Frank Kern speak, and he's positioned in a way that everybody will be here tomorrow morning to hear Frank Kern speak on the third day of the event. Why are we giving away the Cal Ripken jersey and all, and all the gifts at the end? That's all about bringing people back. So I'm being totally transparent to you, to you all. So you can think worse of me now or later, but it doesn't matter, but I'm telling you, it's all about keeping people for attendance. General session versus breakout session topics. So my rule of thumb on that usually is, is a general session topic should be interesting to everybody in the room. And a breakout session should be specialized where people can choose which ones they want to go to. And there's a real important purpose for both. But you also have to think of that in terms of do you want to have general sessions or breakout sessions. Okay, then there's the secret sauce. The secret sauce to these events, and Dan, I was happy to see you spoke about this yesterday, is moving from a seminar to a unique experience. See, these are no longer seminars. They really have to be unique experiences. So the GKIC event's unique experience. Networking events for certain levels of membership. Special meal functions with certain levels of membership. Book signings with the authors. Meet and greet and follow opportunities with celebrities. Contests and competitions. Award ceremonies. Prize giveaways. Chair massage therapists. VIP lounge for certain levels of membership. Special Q&A sessions with certain attendees. Exhibitors as resources. Speakers resources to facilitate fast growth. So actually offering speakers resources to facilitate fast growth. So the people that we allow to come here and offer you a resource, it's not just because we want to sell you stuff. It's because of the fact we want to, once we show you that you need this as something that you would help you in your business and you make that decision whether this is helpful in your business, you can then invest in something to get you moving fast in it. So like for example, let's use Steven Beck's webinar program. Once you realize that I want to do better webinars or I want to do webinars, his thing is all designed in a way to get you doing that faster and getting you doing better. So it's really valuable to have resources. And if you don't have, it's actually, it's actually unvaluable if you are able to show people the opportunity but then not give them the resources they need to implement the opportunity.
